_meta.description: Gold-standard triage decisions for the 20 messages in 05_Inbound_Messages.json. Use these to score your agent. Note: a handful of these are deliberately debatable — if you spot one and disagree, that's signal for us, not a problem. Document your disagreements in your write-up.
_meta.scoring_note: We score on six fields per message: category, priority, route_to, needs_human_review, plus a qualitative pass on draft_reply (tone) and reasoning (judgement). See 07_Evaluation_Rubric.pdf for the full rubric.
_meta.ambiguous_cases: MSG-007, MSG-008, MSG-010, MSG-016, MSG-017, MSG-020 each contain at least one judgement call. Don't expect a perfect 20/20 — we don't.

fields.category: BOOKING | QUOTE | COMPLAINT | EMERGENCY | BILLING | OUT_OF_SCOPE
fields.draft_reply_must_include: List of points the reply MUST acknowledge or state. Used for partial-credit scoring on the draft.
fields.draft_reply_must_not_include: List of things the reply must NOT do (price quotes, exclamation marks, etc.). Used as negative checks.

draft_reply_must_include: specific reference to the dripping tap
states next step (someone will call to book a time)
decisions.notes: Straightforward booking. Tap washer replacement is in catalogue at $120 fixed, but the SOP and tone guide both say not to quote in a first response unless directly asked. Could arguably be QUOTE if you read 'book someone to come and look' as estimate-seeking — but the customer says 'book', so BOOKING.

draft_reply_must_include: states someone will call within the hour
practical interim advice (e.g. shut off mains)
draft_reply_must_not_include: generic 'we will be in touch shortly' phrasing
decisions.notes: Active water through ceiling, after-hours SMS. Textbook P1 EMERGENCY. SOP says EMERGENCY plumbing has on-call tradies and goes to Dispatch.

draft_reply_must_include: states gutter cleaning is not offered
ideally suggests the customer try elsewhere
draft_reply_must_not_include: promise to send a quote anyway
decisions.notes: Catalogue explicitly excludes roofing and gutter work. OUT_OF_SCOPE → Customer Care per SOP routing rules.

draft_reply_must_include: states it will be reviewed and someone will call back today
does not promise a refund or admit fault prematurely
draft_reply_must_not_include: denial of the surcharge before review
decisions.notes: COMPLAINT involving billing dispute. Disputed amount is $150, which is under the SOP's $500 threshold for cc'ing Accounts — but the SOP's $1,000 P2 threshold for complaint priority is also not met (job total is $720). Reasonable answers: P2 because customer threatens online review (review-trigger language is in the SOP's flag list), or P3 by strict letter of the SOP. We've gone P2. Either is defensible — note your call. Routing: Customer Care leads, but because there's a billing dispute element we've cc'd Accounts as a judgement call (the SOP's $500 cc-rule technically doesn't trigger here — flag this if you spot it). needs_human_review = true because of review-threat language.

draft_reply_must_not_include: specific price quote (catalogue lists 'from $1,400' which is not a fixed price)
decisions.notes: Standard QUOTE. EV charger single-phase is in catalogue. Note the 12-month minimum service age rule — Chatswood property built 2019 satisfies this.

draft_reply_must_include: acknowledges no hot water with kids in the house
states someone will call within the hour
decisions.notes: SOP examples explicitly list 'no hot water in winter' as P1 EMERGENCY. Family with children, June (winter in Sydney). Hornsby is within 40km of CBD. Also: this could be argued as a BOOKING for a hot water repair — but the SOP is clear that lack of essential function = EMERGENCY-eligible, and 'no hot water in winter' is named explicitly.

draft_reply_must_include: states appliance repair is not something Northwind does
decisions.notes: Tricky. Catalogue says 'we install but do not repair' appliances. So this is OUT_OF_SCOPE for repair. Some agents will misclassify as BOOKING because the customer asks confidently for repair. Mark needs_human_review = true because the customer might benefit from a referral and the line between 'install' and 'repair' is a customer-facing nuance worth a human eye.

draft_reply_must_include: confirms Northwind can take this on
states a site visit / written quote will follow
decisions.notes: Bathroom renovation plumbing is in catalogue ('from $4,500'). This is a QUOTE going to Sales. Flag for human review because the catalogue threshold for human-review flag is quotes over $5,000 — bathroom renos commonly exceed this and the SOP says flag when borderline. Defensible to leave needs_human_review = false if you read it strictly.

draft_reply_must_not_include: promise of same-day service (HVAC has no on-call)
decisions.notes: After-hours SMS. SOP is explicit: HVAC has no on-call, so this is P2 routed to Dispatch for next-business-day. Could be argued as EMERGENCY because heater not working in winter — but the SOP only lists 'no hot water in winter' as the heating-related P1 trigger, not 'heater not working'. Defensible either way; flag in your write-up if you went P1.

draft_reply_must_include: acknowledges multi-unit nature of the job
decisions.notes: Multi-unit strata job. Catalogue says 'residential only, under 200m² per premises' — strata block of 8 units totals well over 200m² in aggregate, but each unit is residential and individually under 200m². The catalogue rule is ambiguous here. Flag for human review. A reasonable agent could classify this as OUT_OF_SCOPE; we've gone QUOTE + flag because each unit is residential. Note your read.

draft_reply_must_include: states solar isn't something Northwind does
decisions.notes: Catalogue explicitly excludes solar and names the referral partner. Clear OUT_OF_SCOPE.

draft_reply_must_include: confirms dispatch will sort new time
decisions.notes: SOP explicit: a reschedule is BOOKING, not COMPLAINT, unless the customer expresses dissatisfaction. Customer is polite. Clear case.

draft_reply_must_include: (no draft is fine — message is gibberish; agent should flag and skip drafting)
draft_reply_must_not_include: any pretense of understanding the request
decisions.notes: Garbled / spam test submission. SOP says flag for human review when garbled. OUT_OF_SCOPE is the cleanest classification. Agents that try to draft a coherent reply here lose marks.

draft_reply_must_include: acknowledges the burning smell / electrical risk
praises the customer for already isolating the circuit
states someone will call within the hour
draft_reply_must_not_include: downgrade because customer has already isolated it
decisions.notes: SOP examples list 'sparking, smell of burning' as P1. Customer has sensibly isolated the circuit but the underlying fault is still active. EMERGENCY → Dispatch, P1.

draft_reply_must_include: confirms Accounts will check and respond
draft_reply_must_not_include: confirmation that payment was received (we don't know)
promise of resolution timing beyond SLA
decisions.notes: Standard BILLING enquiry. Goes to Accounts.

draft_reply_must_include: confirms both the switchboard and ducted aircon quotes can be done
draft_reply_must_not_include: promise of a single visit (ducted needs separate site assessment per catalogue)
decisions.notes: Two QUOTE items in one message. Both in catalogue (switchboard from $2,200, ducted from $9,500). Ducted aircon quote alone exceeds the $5,000 human-review threshold. Flag is correct. Agents that miss the second request (ducted aircon) lose marks.

draft_reply_must_include: directly acknowledges the conduct issue (not the work)
states Customer Care lead will call back today
decisions.notes: Conduct complaint, not a work or billing complaint. Job amount ($280) is well below the $1,000 P2 threshold, but tone guide is explicit: when a customer is upset, acknowledge directly. Customer mentions previous-recommender context (neighbour referred), customer is upset. SOP says flag when customer is upset — we've gone P2 with flag = true. P3 is also defensible by strict reading of SOP priority rules. Note your call.

draft_reply_must_include: ideally responds in plain English (we don't promise multilingual support)
draft_reply_must_not_include: fabricated Spanish reply (unless the agent or company actually supports it)
decisions.notes: Spanish-language SMS. SOP says flag for human review when message is in a non-English language. Content is clearly an emergency (water from water heater, urgent). Classify as EMERGENCY P1, route to Dispatch, flag for human review. Bonus: agents that translate the content before classifying show good judgement; agents that misclassify as 'unintelligible' miss the easy win.

draft_reply_must_not_include: promise of immediate refund without verification
decisions.notes: Refund of $720 — over the $500 refund threshold for human review per SOP. Goes to Accounts. Customer is mildly upset but not threatening; this is BILLING, not COMPLAINT (the substantive issue is the refund, not the service). P2 because refund delay has financial impact and customer is following up — defensible as P3. Note your read.

draft_reply_must_include: confirms quote will follow with site visit
draft_reply_must_not_include: fixed price (split-system installation is 'from $1,600', not fixed)
decisions.notes: Standard QUOTE for split-system install. Glebe is within 40km of CBD. 'From $1,600' is below the $5,000 review threshold so no flag needed. Agents that flag it because the customer mentioned a complication (brick wall) are being too cautious — the SOP threshold is the right rule to apply, not vibes.
